course_description:
This course provides an understanding of the importance of technological innovation for economic growth. It focuses on how businesses select and develop ideas, the research and development process, collaboration and open innovation, and the management of intellectual property.

The course draws on current insights into how novel business ideas are formed and shaped into promising new products for both existing and new ventures. To create and bring to market innovative ideas, entrepreneurs invest substantial resources in their creation without being certain their idea will be successful. How do entrepreneurs manage this process? What strategies do they use? What role does the innovativeness of their ideas play in the strategies they adopt? How do they maintain motivation when faced with setbacks? In this course, we tackle these questions by focusing on:

By drawing on case studies, andﾠone's own experiences and ideas, students are encouraged to synthesize knowledge throughout the course to gain an in-depth understanding of the complexity of innovation process.

Course Changes in Response to Previous Student Feedback

Students in Sem 1 2026 felt that the final exam placed too much pressure on their memorization of scholarly resources and frameworks used in the course. They also expected assessments to be more practical and experiential in nature given the focus on innovation and entrepreneurship. So, instead of a final exam we have introduced an In-Semester exam that will assess threshold concepts required for idea management. Assessment 3 is now a practical hackathon experience.

course_aims: The course aims to provide students with foundational knowledge on the challenges entrepreneurs face, and the strategies they useﾠto bring innovative products and services to market.ﾠThe course is structured to consider contemporary and emerging theories and practice in innovation and idea management.

learning_outcomes.description: Understand the challenges in creating and implementing innovative ideas.
Demonstrate a thorough understanding of how uncertainty impacts the innovation process.
Demonstrate awareness of how ideas and other forms of intellectual property can be protected.
Apply critical thought skills and course content to real case examples in collaborative teams and present your findings in oral and written form.
Demonstrate knowledge and respect of ethics and ethical standards in relation to global idea management.

assessment_details.title: Practical reflection on innovation
assessment_details.task_description: For this assessment, students will be required to undertake one (1) excursion on Mondays in Weeks 8-9, choosing from one (1) of four (4) excursions available. Students will choose which excursion they can attend and first come, first served basis. These excursions will visit a number of emerging technology firms who are part of Uniquest's portfolio (https://uniquest.com.au). These firms are located in Brisbane and can be accessed by students and the teaching team by public transport. More details will be provided in-class and on Blackboard. The excursions have been designed for students to obtain a practical perspective to the study of innovation and idea management. Students are expected to maintain a notebook/logbook of their excursion using a template provided to students. This notebook/logbook should be used as the basis of a critical reflection upon both the theory and practice of idea management so the quality of note taking, attention to detail, and keen observations will be assessed. This notebook/logbook section is worth 20% and should be included as an appendix to the reflection. Students are required to submit a 1200-word reflection as the body of the submission. The reflection should critically reflect upon the enablers, obstacles, and dynamics of innovation they have observed. and how this connects with scholarly approaches of idea management presented in the course. Students are expected to annotate relevant excursion observations in the body of the reflection, as well as in-text referencing from 3-5 well-justified scholarly references. This reflection section is worth 20%. A template reflection will be provided to students. AI Statement: Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description

Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 14 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: Idea Management Strategy
assessment_details.due_date: Week 12, 10:00 am This assessment will be administered in the timetabled seminar
assessment_details.other_conditions: Peer assessed. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: This assignment encourages students to apply course concepts and theories in a hackathon style experience delivered during the Week 12 seminar. This is a practical exercise for students who in teams will appraise an emerging technology and provide recommendation on how to bring this technology to the market and to develop strategies for greater market penetration and/or coherence. Student teams must also consider ethical considerations when engaging a customer groups, organisations, and the community in general. Students will be randomly formed into teams of five (5) students in Week 11 and are expected to collaborate and communicate together in the hackathon experience in Week 12. Student teams will be provided a client brief at the beginning of the seminar and are expected to rigorously discuss the client brief, appraise opportunities and challenges, and complete a Business Model Canvas (BMC) as a deliverable of this hackathon experience. Additionally, the team should choose one (1) team member who will give a 2-minute elevator pitch of their proposed solution and strategy to the teaching team. Each team member is expected to make an equal contribution in the hackathon. At the end of the presentation, all team members will be required to complete a peer assessment of each other's performance and contribution to the evaluation. Instructions for how to do this will be provided in class. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Students will be provided with relevant materials in the seminar. These materials will be collected at the end of the seminar.

Late submission
Late submissions are not accepted for in-class presentations. Failure to present at the scheduled time will result in a mark of zero for this assessment.

learning_activities.topic: Course introduction, Entrepreneurship, and the BMC Overview of the course, entrepreneurship, and idea management implementation through the Business Model Canvas (BMC) Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L05
Innovation and Ideas What does it mean to be innovative? Where do innovative ideas come from? Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L05
Types & Patterns of innovation Types & Patterns of innovation & an appraisal of 'creative destruction' Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L05

Understanding and Strategizing for Uncertainty The risk & uncertainty of innovation. Discussion of dominant designs and first-mover advantages and disadvantages Identifying core competence, dynamic capabilities & a firm's current position to exploit an opportunity Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L05
From Uncertainty to Advantage: R&D, IP, and Collaborative Execution R&D strategies: Converting Uncertainty into Knowledge IP Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05

In-Semester exam/ Discussing new product development teams and collaboration strategies An invigilated in-person exam will be held at the start of the seminar. Discussing new product development teams and collaboration strategies: How to exploit tangible and intangible sources of value effectively Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 7 To Week 9
learning_activities.topic: Innovation excursions Weeks 7-9 are focussed on experiencing idea management in real life. In Week 7, we will have a guest lecture from Uniquest and an on-campus excursion to help prepare students for off-site excursion in the timetabled seminar slot of 10am-1pm. Students are then required to attend one (1) of four (4) excursions planned across Weeks 8-9. On the Monday of Week 8, two excursions will leave from St Lucia campus at 8am, visiting up to two firms in Brisbane and aiming to return to campus by 1pm. On the Monday of Week 9, two excursions will leave from St Lucia campus at 8am, visiting up to two firms in Brisbane and aiming to return to campus by 1pm. Each excursion will be capped at a specific number of students. Students will be allocated a spot on the excursion on a first come, first served basis. A Padlet will be provided for sign-up. The purpose of the field trip is to experience the opportunities and the challenges of idea management first hand and visiting firms in the Uniquest portfolio More details about the logistics of these field trips will be provided in class. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

Practical reflection preparation Labour Day Public Holiday - Monday 4th May 2026 Students are encouraged to prepare their practical reflection for submission (A2) based on their excursion Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

Managing emerging technologies and reintroductioin of the Business Model Canvas (BMC) Understanding the obstacles to emerging technologies and the need for stakeholder engagement Discussion of the optimal size, structural and cultural dimensions of a firm that seeks to innovate Re-introduction of the BMC to help prepare students for the Week 12 hackathon Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
Hackathon Working in teams, students are expected to develop an idea management for a case study firm Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

Idea management and the entrepreneurial mindset Students will be provided a capstone overview of the course and an opportunity to reflect upon learning activities and their own entrepreneurial mindsets Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05
